text:
War in the Time of Social Media: Journalists’ Account of the 2014 Israel-Gaza War This study seeks to explore some of the unprecedented complexities that journalists may encounter in war zones such as in the case of the Israel–Gaza war. Based on interviews with 18 journalists working in Gaza, this qualitative study delves into the challenges that journalists face while reporting from war-zones. It applies the theory of the Hierarchy of Influences Model (HAM) to examine individual, media, social, institutional and ideological variables that affect the way that the war is reported and released to the international media.

The study found a “semi-hierarchical” relationship between the microworlds of individual’s everyday lives and the macro-world of societal structures. The study highlights a very high level of physical and psychological burden on local journalists, who are forced to act as both expert observers and themselves victims of the violence they report. The main themes highlighted in the study include the attempts of local journalists to secure their own safety and professional integrity in a context of poor data availability, how social media, which has become a vital communication channel, at the same time censors content through different algorithms, and the conflict between journalistic objectivity and national pride.

This research is a step towards linking media theory and research more closely with the everyday reality of journalists working on the war front. It will contribute significantly to the pedagogic, policy-making and practice spheres of journalism and media studies, calling for a more comprehensive range of protective measures, psychological services and codes of journalism practice that correspond to the reality of war reporting. In the same time, this study is contributing to the “unbroken stories” behind the broken bodies and souls of war journalists. Collectively, these stories are part of a global conversation on the issues of journalists’ safety and freedom and the role of media in war-torn societies.
